text: The surgical robot is docked, and the camera along with instruments are introduced into the surgical field for a robotic total thyroidectomy via bilateral axillary breast approach. The thirty-degree endoscope is inserted through the right axillary port, providing a view from a lateral, inferior, and anterior angle over the surgical field. The anterior border of the right sternocleidomastoid muscle and the neck midline are outlined, with the flap already raised.

The surgeon manipulates three robotic arms: the PK Dissector with bipolar energy in the left hand through the right axillary port, a cutting instrument (monopolar hook or harmonic) in the right hand through the left breast port, and a bipolar fenestrated forceps (prograsp) in the fourth arm through the left axillary port, assisting in tissue exposure. The camera is positioned between the surgeon's hands.

The surgeon reaches the lateral border of the thyroid lobe and uses a monopolar hook as a probe to locate the recurrent laryngeal nerve. The hook, connected to intraoperative nerve monitoring equipment, maps the nerve between anatomical landmarks, with surface electrodes placed in the patient's endotracheal tube.

The surgeon carefully follows the thyroid capsule to detach the gland from its attachments, ensuring protection of the parathyroid glands and sealing vascular structures. The lower pole of the thyroid is detached from the trachea while dissecting the left central compartment.

The surgeon uses a soaked cotinoid to protect the nerve from thermal injury while dissecting closer to the trachea and the ligament of Bary. The thyroid lobe is further dissected from the trachea, leaving only the superior neurovascular pedicle attached. The superior thyroid pole is freed from the trachea, and the lobe remains attached to the superior pedicle. The superior laryngeal nerve is identified using a probe, and the superior thyroid vein and artery are selectively ligated with the harmonic scalpel.

The specimen is placed over the right sternocleidomastoid muscle for later removal. A cotinoid is used to protect the right recurrent laryngeal nerve during dissection in the central compartment. The specimen is again stored over the ECM. The focus shifts to the left lobe of the thyroid, where medial traction from the isthmus is applied, followed by dissection of the lobe. The recurrent laryngeal nerve is identified during this process.
The surgeon identifies the superior laryngeal nerve and ligates the vascular superior pedicle using surgical instruments.

The surgical field is irrigated with saline, and hemostasis is checked on both sides. Hemostatic glue and fibrillar Surgicel are applied. The strap muscles are sutured in the midline using a barbed V-lock suture. A drain is placed, exiting through the left axillary incision.
The surgical procedure leaves no visible scars.
